It was part of the whole and deep history and pattern of driving inequality through housing practices.

Housing in real estate has been one of the most significant in enduring drivers of structural racism and racial inequality in the history of the United States, going back to the aftermath of the Civil War.

So many of you are likely familiar with some effective segregationist policies in the United States in the 20th century, particularly in the Jim Crow South, which forbid African Americans from using the same facilities as whites, buses, bathrooms, and pools.
Maybe you've learned about the role of the Civil Rights Act or the Civil Rights Movement in basically rendering that illegal.

But this was also a story simultaneously of how neighborhoods in the urban north that didn't have the same Jim Crow laws became increasingly segregated through sets of other more subtle forms of practices.
In some cases in the case of housing it was driven by private real estate industry and connection with government actors.
So, is segregation increased across the first half of the 20th century as part of the two ways of the Great Migration of African Americans from the South into the urban centers of the North?
And as those urban centers received them, they developed practices for segregating.

Although it was illegal for governments to actively segregate neighborhoods according to race, to use that as a good reason that you can or can't live in a certain area, other methods were available for essentially achieving the same ends in practice.
So again, facial neutral laws, but there were other sorts of social practices that enabled that segregation to take place anyways.
One of the most notorious practices was what has come to be known is redlining.
Drawing maps of cities that distinguish neighborhoods in terms of relative risk.
Redlining was performed by federal housing agencies during the Great Depression in the 1930s in order to increase home ownership for lower-income Americans, or at least white-lower-income Americans.
The areas coded as highest risk, colored in red, hence the name of redlining, were deemed too risky for the federal government to ensure banks issuing mortgages in those areas.
So it became especially difficult to access loans. and buy a home.
As these neighborhoods were disproportionately black, this meant that credit was near impossible to come by for African Americans looking to own a house.
And the effects of these practices has continued to resonate today.

And in a number of jurisdictions, including in the Bay Area and in Berkeley and Chicago, if you look at redlining masks, you can actually map them on to demographics today with a surprising amount of fidelity.
Now the origins of this practice of redlining, it wasn't simply a top-down government initiative to racially segregate.
We're actually lay in the data that the federal government was using.
And that came out of a change in data practices, basically early data science techniques, developed by the private real estate industry in the 1920s.

In other words, segregation was established and reinforced in part through the work of real estate professionals who were concerned with establishing reliable methods for predicting the value of a home that it was part of professionalizing a certain kind of data science practice is and and generating new forms of making data that actually underwrote the federal housing system that was captured in these redlining maps.

And again, was this the real estate industry was professionalized in a few places in this, uh, in a, uh, at first one of those places was not accidentally Chicago in the first half of the 20th century.

The men that were part of these industries aimed to standardize procedures for assessing property values, that is to make more objective, precise, accurate mathematical, the determination of like, what do you think a home is worth so we can go and better sell it?
They wanted property appraisal to be a kind of empirical and rigorous data science guided by strict methods and principles.
